ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC represents boiled hen's egg; used as closest parent as no distinct soft-boiled sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 browser.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 is the closest parent concept for edible egg substance; no specific soft-boiled egg concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one large soft-boiled egg weighing approximately 50 g, as commonly served as a ramen topping or standalone snack.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, boiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173424)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for whole boiled egg used as the primary nutrient reference. Soft-boiling versus hard-boiling produces negligible differences in macro and micronutrient composition per 100 g; values are directly applicable. Sodium reflects plain boiled egg without added seasoning; restaurant versions (e.g. ajitsuke tamago) may have higher sodium due to soy-sauce marinade.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Eggs contain negligible digestible carbohydrate (1.12 g per 100 g), making a formal GI measurement inapplicable. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature consistently classify eggs as having an effectively zero glycemic response. GI is recorded as 0 (confirmed zero carbohydrate-driven glycemic response) and gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 50 g serving = (0 x 0.56) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content; low in carbohydrate
hypertension.guidance: A plain soft-boiled egg contains approximately 62 mg sodium per 50 g serving, which is acceptable within a hypertension-friendly diet; however, marinated ramen-style versions (ajitsuke tamago) can contain significantly more sodium due to soy-sauce brine and should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate; high-quality protein supports satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.12 g of carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic index of effectively zero, soft-boiled eggs have no meaningful impact on postprandial blood glucose and are a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (373 mg per 100 g); beneficial unsaturated fats also present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Eggs are high in dietary cholesterol; current evidence suggests moderate egg consumption (up to one egg per day) is acceptable for most individuals with cardiovascular disease, but those with dyslipidaemia should consult a clinician regarding frequency, as saturated fat (3.27 g per 100 g) may modestly influence LDL cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A soft-boiled egg is a whole hen's egg cooked in simmering water for approximately 6–7 minutes, resulting in a fully set egg white and a warm, jammy, semi-liquid yolk. In Japanese cuisine, the soft-boiled egg is most commonly encountered as hanjuku tamago (半熟卵), often marinated in a soy sauce, mirin, and dashi mixture to produce ajitsuke tamago (味付け卵) — a savoury, umami-rich ramen topping with a golden-brown exterior. The texture contrast between the silky white and the creamy, flowing yolk is a defining characteristic. Nutritionally, a 50 g egg provides approximately 77 kcal, 6.3 g of high-quality complete protein containing all essential amino acids, 5.3 g of fat (including beneficial monounsaturated fats), and negligible carbohydrate, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary patterns. Eggs are a rich source of choline, vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and vitamin A. The cholesterol content (approximately 186 mg per egg) is notable but current dietary guidelines do not restrict egg consumption for most healthy adults. In Singapore, soft-boiled eggs are also a beloved breakfast staple, typically served in a cup with dark soy sauce and white pepper, distinct from the Japanese ramen preparation.
common_user_queries: how many calories in a soft boiled egg
is soft boiled egg good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for whole boiled egg (FDC ID 173424), an approved primary source. Soft-boiling versus hard-boiling produces no meaningful compositional difference per 100 g. GI classification is well-supported by peer-reviewed literature confirming negligible glycemic response for eggs. Japanese local-language names are verified standard terms.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; eggs contain no dietary fibre by nature (naturally zero). Vitamin C is 0.0 mg — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; eggs contain no vitamin C (naturally zero). Trans fat is 0.03 g — trace amount of naturally occurring vaccenic/conjugated linoleic acid from animal source, not industrially produced trans fat. Glycemic index is recorded as 0 because eggs contain negligible digestible carbohydrate and produce no measurable glycemic response; gi_category is set to 'low' per convention for foods with effectively zero GI. Glycemic load per 50 g serving is 0.0. Sodium value (124 mg per 100 g) reflects plain boiled egg; marinated ajitsuke tamago versions served in ramen restaurants will have substantially higher sodium. The input description referenced 'traditional padang flavor' which is inconsistent with a Japanese soft-boiled egg and has been disregarded as menu marketing copy. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173424.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (confirmed zero, naturally zero for eggs), sodium_mg 124.0, saturated_fat_g 3.27, sugar_g 1.12 — all present as numeric values from approved source.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 0 (confirmed zero glycemic response for eggs per peer-reviewed GI literature), gi_category 'low', glycemic_load_per_serving 0.0, gi_source present citing Atkinson et al. AJCN 2021 and University of Sydney GI Database.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 (soft-boiled egg food product) is an exact match. FoodEx2 A00LC used as closest_parent with documented gap note. SNOMED CT 102263004 used as closest_parent with documented gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters and covers food identity, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context (Japanese ramen and Singapore breakfast), clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes'.
